input_row.title: Ensure 'Allowed Protocols' for shared access signature (SAS) tokens is set to 'HTTPS Only'
input_row.description: Shared access signatures (SAS) can be used to grant limited access to Azure Storage resources. When generating a SAS, it is possible to specify the allowed protocols for a request made with the SAS. It is recommended to allow requests over HTTPS only.
input_row.rationale: If a SAS is passed over HTTP and intercepted, an attacker performing a man-in-the- middle attack can read the SAS. Then, they can use that SAS just as the intended user could have. This can potentially compromise sensitive data or allow for data corruption by the malicious user. Impact: SAS can pose security risks if they are not managed carefully.
input_row.audit: It is not possible to audit generated SAS.
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal If SAS have been created to allow HTTP and were created with a stored access policy (SAP), the SAS can be revoked by deleting the SAP or updating the SAP expiration time to a time in the past: 1. Go to Storage accounts. 2. Click on a storage account. 3. Under Data storage, click Queues. 4. Click the three dots next to a queue. 5. Click Access policy. 6. Click the three dots next to an access policy. 7. Click Delete. 8. Click Save. 9. Repeat steps 1-8 as needed to revoke SAS created with SAP.  If SAS have been created to allow HTTP and were not created with a SAP, the SAS can be revoked by regenerating the storage account access keys: Note: Regenerating access keys can affect any applications or Azure services that are dependent on the storage account key. 1. Go to Storage accounts. 2. Click on a storage account. 3. Under Security + networking, click Access keys. 4. Next to each key, click Rotate key. 5. Click Yes to confirm. 6. Repeat steps 1-5 as needed to revoke SAS. Default Value: When generating a SAS, the default selection for Allowed protocols is HTTPS only. References: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-sas-overview 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-account-keys- manage Additional Information: This recommendation is based on the recommendation Ensure 'Allowed Protocols' for shared access signature (SAS) tokens is set to 'HTTPS Only', from the Common Reference Recommendations > Secrets and Keys > Shared Access Signatures section.
input_row.impact: SAS can pose security risks if they are not managed carefully.
input_row.reference: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-sas-overview 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-account-keys- manage Additional Information: This recommendation is based on the recommendation Ensure 'Allowed Protocols' for shared access signature (SAS) tokens is set to 'HTTPS Only', from the Common Reference Recommendations > Secrets and Keys > Shared Access Signatures section.

gpt_response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Access the Azure Portal:**
   - Go to the Azure Portal (https://portal.azure.com/).
   
2. **Navigate to Storage Accounts:**
   - Search for and select "Storage accounts" from the menu or the homepage.

3. **Select a Storage Account:**
   - Click on the storage account you want to review.

4. **Check Queues Access Policies:**
   - Under the "Data storage" section, click on "Queues."
   - Locate the queue you want to inspect and click on the three dots next to it.
   - Click on "Access policy."

5. **Review Access Policies:**
   - For each access policy listed, click the three dots next to it and choose "Edit."
   - Verify the "Allowed protocols" setting to ensure it is set to "HTTPS Only."

6. **Delete or Update Policies as Necessary:**
   - If you find any access policies that allow HTTP, you can either delete them or modify the associated stored access policies (SAP) to restrict them to HTTPS only.
   - If the SAS has been created without a stored access policy and allows HTTP, you'll need to regenerate the storage account access keys.

7. **Verify Secure Configuration:**
   - The expected secure configuration should be that the "Allowed protocols" for all SAS tokens have been correctly set to "HTTPS Only."

8. **Confirm Compliance:**
   - Ensure that no SAS options are allowing HTTP. If all queues have their configurations set to HTTPS only and any non-compliant SAS tokens have been revoked, compliance is confirmed.
